student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: The Numbers Behind the Project -- Presenting KPIs, Milestones and Budget Deviations
lesson.menu_desc: The Line 4 programme board wants the numbers, not the story. Ingrid Vogel, the finance director on the German partner's side, has the dashboard open before you speak: a KPI in the amber, a cost variance above threshold, the axle-counter package come in over budget. Tonight you learn to present numbers the way a board reads them -- the vocabulary of project controls (KPI, burn rate, run rate, forecast, earned value, cost variance, drawdown, threshold, to come in over/under budget), the idioms that keep it human (a ballpark figure, to break even, the bottom line, to move the needle), and the grammar that turns a number into a commitment: the future perfect and the future continuous -- 'By the end of Q3 we will have closed the variance', 'This time next month the crew will be running the SATs'. You read a real status report the way Ingrid reads it, and you present the deviation to her live: lead with the bottom line, own the overrun, and forecast the recovery in dates you will hit -- 40 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 17 -- The Numbers Behind the Project
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 17 -- The Numbers Behind the Project
lesson.grammar_point: the future perfect and the future continuous (by the end of Q3 we will have closed the variance / this time next month the crew will be running the SATs)

listenings.text: João, before you present the numbers to the programme board tomorrow, let me tell you how a board actually listens, because engineers get this exactly backwards. You want to walk them through the whole story, the context, the cause, and arrive at the number at the end, like a proof. Do not. A board reads the number first and the story second. So lead with the bottom line. In the first sentence, tell them where the project stands against plan and against budget, one KPI, one figure. Then, and only then, the why. Second thing: own the deviation before they find it. You have a cost variance above threshold, the axle-counter package came in over budget. Say so, plainly, in your own voice, before Ingrid points at it on the dashboard. The number that the presenter names first is a number under control; the number the board discovers is a number out of control, even when it is the same number. Third, and this is where your grammar earns its keep, a forecast is not a hope, it is a commitment, so say it like one. Do not say we hope to finish the tests by the third quarter. Say by the end of Q3 we will have completed the site acceptance tests, and by the time the board meets again we will have closed the variance. That is the future perfect, and it lands as a promise with a date on it. And when they ask what the team is doing right now to recover, use the future continuous: this time next month the commissioning crew will be running the SATs on nights. It tells them the recovery is already in motion, not a plan on a slide. Lead with the bottom line, own the overrun in your own words, and forecast in dates you will actually hit. The numbers do not defend themselves. You do.

Three things about reading project numbers, from someone who has built a lot of these dashboards. First, know the difference between the burn rate and the run rate, because people mix them and it costs them credibility. The burn rate is how fast you are spending right now, this month's drawdown from the budget. The run rate is that pace projected forward, what the whole year looks like if this month simply continues. A board does not want this month's spend; it wants the run rate, because the run rate tells them where you land. Second, earned value is the one number that keeps you honest. It is not how much you have spent and it is not how much time has passed; it is the budgeted value of the work you have actually finished. You can be halfway through the calendar and halfway through the money and still be badly behind, because only a third of the work is truly done. The cost variance is the gap between what that finished work was supposed to cost and what it actually cost, and once it crosses the threshold, it goes red on the board and someone has to explain it. Third, treat a ballpark figure and a forecast as two completely different animals. A ballpark is a rough number you give early, when nobody should hold you to it, and you must say out loud that it is a ballpark. A forecast is a commitment, updated with real data, and you state it in the future perfect: by quarter end we will have closed the gap. If you present a ballpark as if it were a forecast, you will spend the next three meetings explaining why the real number moved. Lead with the bottom line, know whether a number moves the needle or not, and never promise in the future perfect anything you have not actually planned to deliver.

patterns.title: The future perfect and the future continuous -- two lenses on the same future
cards: Use it for an action that will already be COMPLETE by a deadline. "By the end of Q3, we will have completed the SATs." "By the time the board meets again, we will have closed the variance." It answers the board's real question -- how much will be DONE by then -- and it lands as a promise with a date on it, not a hope. The verb after have is a past participle: completed, closed, finished -- not the base form.
In progress AT a future point

Use it for an action that will be ONGOING at or around a moment in the future. "This time next month, the crew will be running the SATs on nights." "Throughout the commissioning window, the team will be working two shifts." It tells the board the recovery is already in motion -- something happening at that moment, not a plan on a slide.
BY, not UNTIL, with the future perfect

The future perfect takes BY for its deadline -- the point a thing is done by. "By December we will have closed the gap" (not "until December"). UNTIL is for something that CONTINUES up to a point ("we will keep testing until December"). Portuguese uses ate for both, so the reflex is to say "until" -- and it breaks the sentence. The deadline of a completed action is always BY.
Why it sounds senior in a numbers meeting

"We hope to finish by Q3" is a wish; "by Q3 we will have finished" is a commitment with a date. The future perfect lets you promise a completed STATE by a moment; the future continuous shows the work is already underway. Together they turn a forecast into something the board can hold you to -- which is exactly what a board wants to hear.

patterns.text: One reflex runs through both: look at what comes after will. will HAVE + a past participle (completed, closed, delivered) = finished by a point. will BE + an -ing (running, testing, working) = in progress at a point. The moment you hear yourself say "we will complete by Q3" for something that must be DONE by then, upgrade it to "we will have completed" -- the plain future says it happens sometime around Q3; the future perfect says it is finished by Q3. In a budget review, that difference is the difference between a hope and a commitment.

items.situation: Ingrid opens: "Just give me where we stand -- don't walk me through the whole project." Lead with the bottom line in one sentence.
tips: The bottom line: we're on schedule and two percent over budget, driven by one package -- the axle counters. Everything else is on plan.
In one line: milestone-wise we're green, cost-wise we're amber -- a single cost variance above threshold, and I'll take you straight to it.

items.situation: There is a cost variance above threshold on the dashboard. Own it before she points at it -- name the number in your own words.
tips: Before you ask -- yes, the axle-counter package came in over budget; the variance is above threshold and it's mine to explain.
Let me name it first: we have a cost variance of four percent on one package. It's above the threshold, it's red on your screen, and here's why.

items.situation: She asks when the tests will be finished. Commit to a deadline using the future perfect.
tips: By the end of Q3 we will have completed the site acceptance tests -- that's a date, not a hope.
By the time the board meets again, we will have closed the variance and finished the SATs on Line 4.

items.situation: She asks what the team is doing right now to recover the schedule. Describe the ongoing work with the future continuous.
tips: This time next month the commissioning crew will be running the SATs on nights, so we recover two weeks without touching daytime traffic.
Through August the team will be working two shifts on the interlocking -- the recovery is already in motion, not a plan on a slide.

items.situation: She wants a rough completion cost off the top of your head, before the reforecast is done. Give a number but protect yourself.
tips: As a ballpark figure, somewhere around six percent over on that package -- but that's a ballpark, not the forecast; I'll have the reforecast by Friday.
Off the top of my head, a ballpark would be six percent; I'd rather give you the firm forecast once the earned-value data is in.

items.situation: She challenges a small saving you're proud of: "Does that really change anything?" Tell her whether it moves the needle.
tips: Honestly, that one doesn't move the needle -- it's a rounding difference. The number that matters is the axle-counter variance, and that's where I'm focused.
It helps us break even on the minor packages, but it doesn't move the needle on the bottom line -- so I'm not spending the board's time on it.

bank.label: Leading with the bottom line
items: The bottom line: schedule green, cost amber -- one variance above threshold, and I'll take you straight to it.
Where we stand: on plan against the milestones, two percent over against budget on a single package.
One KPI, one figure: earned value is at sixty-two percent, and that's the number that matters tonight.

bank.label: Owning the deviation
items: Before you ask -- yes, the axle-counter package came in over budget; the cost variance is above threshold and it's mine to explain.
Let me name it first: a four-percent cost variance on one package, red on your dashboard, here's the cause.
I'd rather flag the drawdown myself than have you find it -- the burn rate spiked in April when we expedited the units.

bank.label: Forecasting with the future perfect / continuous
items: By the end of Q3 we will have completed the site acceptance tests and closed the variance.
By the time the board meets again, we will have brought the run rate back inside plan.
This time next month the commissioning crew will be running the SATs on nights to recover the two weeks.

bank.label: Ballpark vs forecast (protecting a number)
items: As a ballpark figure it's around six percent over -- but that's a ballpark, not the forecast; the reforecast lands Friday.
I'd rather give you the firm forecast once the earned-value data is in than commit to a number tonight.
That saving doesn't move the needle -- it's a rounding difference; the axle-counter variance is where the money is.
